content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To       : Robert Summers                                Date : Oct. 14, 1976
           Franchise Tax Board


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        Richard Eichman

Subject: LOBBYIST Reporting

       The staff has considered your memorandum of August 25,
1976, and the attachments thereto and has concluded that the
advice that a general lobbyist expense is not "paid" at the
time the credit card is presented is correct.  The staff agrees
that it may be easier for a filer and an auditor if your sug-
gested method were followed, but we think that under present
regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18615(b)(1)(B) the Com-
mission cannot require a lobbyist to report a credit card
transaction in the month incurred.

       2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18615(b)(1)(A) states that
"activity expenses" shall be reported "in the report for that
period in which the expense is incurred, regardless of when
... paid."  On the other hand, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18615(b)(1)(B) states that "general lobbying expenses ... may
be reported when paid and need not be accrued." These two sub-
sections set forth different reporting requirements depending
upon the nature of the expenditure and clearly indicate that
general lobbying expenses may be reported when paid and need
not be reported when incurred.  Furthermore, because the two
subsections contrast "incurring" with "paying" an
expense we think the regulation contemplates that a credit
card expenditure is paid when the charging party pays the bill,
not when he incurs the charge by the card.  Accordingly,
we do not believe we can change our original advice without an
amendment to the existing regulation.

       This issue points to another problem created by
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18615 and inconsistencies between
the forms and the regulations.  We think that the appropriate
time to resolve these problems is when the accounting regula-
tions are reviewed by the Commission in the coming months.  At
that time, all recommendations by the Franchise Tax Board will be
solicited and considered.

       In the interim, we think that lobbyists may report
general lobbying expenses when incurred or paid (to the credit
company).  If you have any further questions or comments, please
contact me.

       RE:jo

"State of California                                          Franchise Tax Board

Memorandum

To     :  Rich Eichman                              Date  :  August 25, 1976
         Fair Political Practices Commission
         1100 K Street                             File No.:  RCS:1r
         Sacramento, CA  95814


From   :  Robert C. Summers

Subject:  Lobbyist Reporting


         We are having some difficulty with Conclusion No. 2 regarding definitions
of payment involving a credit card.  Our audit and legal staffs both
disagree with your interpretation.  Attached are copies of memos from
Dan Converse and Doug Dick relating their concerns about this question.
I have also attached a copy of a memo from Rudy Nothenberg indicating the
advice he was giving to lobbyists on this same question.

         It is clear in the memo and the regulations that any activity expense
which is charged by credit card must be reported when the expense is
incurred.  It seems to me that it is easier for a filer to record the
transaction, maintain the appropriate substantiation, i.e., the receipt,
and comply with the law by reporting all of his credit card transactions
when the expense is incurred regardless of how the expense is finally paid.

         I would appreciate it if the staff would re-evaluate their answer and
consider the issues that we have raised in these memos.  Perhaps a regulation
should be proposed covering Section 82044 if the questions cannot be resolved.
Also attached is a copy of memo from our legal staff.


                    [signature]

                    Chief, Political Reform Audit Division

Attachments

cc:  D. Converse
     D. Dick
     N. Fuller


[On the left side of the page, there appears to be text written vertically in blue/purple ink that reads:]

IT
U
b
C

[followed by additional characters that are not clearly legible]

State of California                                                 Franchise Tax Board

Memorandum

To      :   Robert C. Summere, Chief                    Date    :   August 13, 1976
            Political Reform Audit Division
                                                        File No.:   DO:jmt
                                                                    A-9

From    :   Dan Converse                                           RECEIVED
                                                                   AUG 16, 1976
Subject :   FPPC Interpretation of Lobbyist Audit Criteria        POLITICAL REFORM AUDIT DIV.
                                                                   FRANCHISE TAX BOARD


            I just received a memorandum from Ken Waldrum, dated August 9, which
            indicates that the comments expressed by Rich Eichman in the memorandum,
            dated July 23, "should be utilized in future lobbyist audits."

            I am willing to follow Item 1 (Agency Involved) in Rich's memo, but am
            adamantly opposed to the position he states for Item 2 (Credit Card
            Expenditures) for the following reasons:

                  1.   It contradicts the intent of the Act to provide timely
                       disclosure of lobbyist expenses.

                  2.   It contradicts Section 82044 which defines payment.

                  3.   It contradicts the definition of payment as applied in
                       general legal practice.

                  4.   It contradicts the FPPC's own instructions for Schedule (i)
                       printed on the lobbyist monthly report forms (620 and 625).

                  5.   It contradicts the FPPC's new Instruction Manual for
                       Lobbyists (Page 8), dated June 1976.

                  6.   It is extremely difficult, if not impossible, to determine
                       when any particular expense has been paid to a credit card
                       company, unless the entire balance of the account is paid
                       in full. There are problems with method (FIFO, LIFO),
                       service charges, and splitting of payments (pays $20,
                       smallest expense is $30).

            I recommend that we do not follow Item 2 and that we get the FPPC staff to
            reverse their opinion. If this is not possible, we should not follow

Bob Summers
p. 2
August 16, 1976

credit card company has a loan to the lobbyist outstanding. Mastercharge
sends it's customers "checks" which can be used to pay income or property
taxes, and if insurance we could not disallow a property tax deduction on
the 540 if the taxpayer paid it with Mastercharge and paid the Master-
charge back next year. In fact, we would allow the deduction and any interest
expense during the interim.

I fully support a rediscussion of this matter with the Commission.

Douglas E. Dick
Director, Southern District
Political Reform Audit Division

cc: D. Converse
N. Fuller
J. Pavalasky
K. Waldram
So. Administrators
K. Evans

State of California                                                    Franchise Tax Board

Memorandum

To    :   Bob Summers, Chief                          Date   :   August 16, 1976
         Political Reform Audit Division
                                                       File No.:  ELM PR:DED:en

From  :   Doug Dick

Subject:  FPPC Interpretation of Lobbyist Audit Criteria

         Dan sent me a copy of his response to Ken Waldrum's cover memo accom-
panying Rich Eichman's letter dated July 23, 1976.

         On the face this seems like the type of thing which is no big deal.....
i.e., why should we be concerned with non-activity expenses, and whether
they are required to be reported when incurred or paid.

         The problem with credit card accounts, however, is we cannot determine
payment of a specific item.  An account of $1,000.00 only requires pay-
ment of $50.00, and which $50.00 has been paid?  This is further com-
pounded by mixing personal and lobbying expenses.

         Treating all credit card expenditures as "payments" at the time the ex-
pense is incurred is so simple to work with, we should encourage the
Commission to make their decision to further the working of the Act,
and not on strict business law principles.  What does it matter when a
credit card expenditure is "legally" an expenditure, or that if the
lobbyist never pays the amount he never really had the expense.....or
other legal arguments.  The important items are (1) the lobbyist engaged
in an activity constituting a general lobbying expense, (2) this is re-
portable, (3) the auditor needs to be able to identify a required re-
porting date.

         If the Commission requires the expense to be reported when incurred or
when "billed", the auditor can work with this to determine proper report-
ing.  If the auditor is to work with a "payment" date, we need rules on
partial payment.....i.e., first in, first out, or some such rule.  It
will take a lobbyist's time as well as ours if we have difficult rules
to work with.

         I actually do not agree that an expense has not been "paid" when a credit
card is used.  The business establishment receives their money and the

Robert C. Summers
August 13, 1976
Page 2


Item 2 until after the FPPC revises the lobbyist forms, instruction
booklet, and issues a regulation on how to determine which expenses have
been paid when a payment is for less than the balance of the credit
card account.

[signature]
Director, Northern District
Political Reform Audit Division

cc: Doug Dick
Norman Fuller
John Pavalasky
Ken Waldrum
N. Administrators


POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
BOX 1610 #12
RENO, NSVII
702-885-5632


April 11, 1975


Mr. James Edward Lombardo
925 L Street, Suite 920
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Lombardo:

We have had an opportunity to briefly review your
filing for the month of February and have a point of
clarification we should like to make.

When you report your disbursements under Part 2 of
the form it is necessary that you show the actual name of
the person from whom the services are acquired rather than
the name of the credit card company to whom the payment is
made.

Since there are only two items of over $10 which are
involved on the February report, neither of which involve
a designated official, I would think that a correction
by letter to the Secretary of State would be sufficient.

You might remember that the regulations provide that
you list the expense for this type of item as it is incurred,
showing the name of the purveyor at the time of the filing of
the report and that the ultimate payment to the credit card
company need not again be reported by you.

If you have any questions about any of the foregoing
please feel free to call upon us.

Very truly yours,

Rudy Rothenberg
Consultant

State of California
Memorandum

To : Robert Summers                          Date : August 25, 1976

From : Timothy W. Boyer                      File No.: 410:TWB:hr

Subject: FPPC Interpretation of Lobbyist Audit Criteria.

The reasoning expressed in Dan Converse's memo of August 13, 1976 should
be supported. The FPPC staff does not appear to appreciate the lack of
disclosure that is possible by minimum partial payment of outstanding
credit card accounts. Further, the staff opinion seems inconsistent with
a number of disclosure provisions of the FRA including §86107(b)(4).
That section intended a disbursement covering more than one item to be
reported as if a separate disbursement had been made for each item.
Such intent should be applicable to the credit card situation, also.

If the staff is unreceptive to change, it should be suggested their position
be advanced in regulation form to be considered by and become a responsi-
bility of the Commission.


Timothy W. Boyer
Counsel

cc: Mr. Bruce W. Walker


Additional cc's: D. Converse
                D. Dick
                N. Fuller


- nic

Franchise Tax Board

orandum

Dan Converse
Doug Dick
John Pavalusky

Date : August 9, 1976

File No.: EW:ks
A-9

From : Ken Waldrum

Subject: FPPC Interpretation of Lobbyist Audit Criteria

Attached is a memo from the Commission outlining their staff
conclusions on required reporting of agencies lobbied and
audit sources for lobbyist. These comments should be
utilized in future lobbyist audits. Note the deviations
from current policy regarding credit card payments.

Questions and comments should be directed to Ken Waldrum.

Ken Waldrum
Administrative Assistant
Program Coordination
Political Reform Audit Division

att.

cc. Norman L. Fuller


California

emorandum

To : Norm Fuller
Franchise Tax Board                                Date : July 23, 1976


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Rich Eichman

Subject: Lobbyist Reporting


Recently, you presented the following statements to the Commission:

1. According to Section 86107(f), the agencies lobbied must be
   reported.

2. The definition of payment for credit card accounts is at the
   time the charge is made. Therefore, the incurrence and pay-
   ment of the expense are at the same date and the filer has
   no choice as to when to report it.

The staff has discussed the above statements and reached the follow-
ing conclusions with respect to each:

1. A lobbyist must disclose an agency in "Agency Involved", Form
   620, only when the lobbyist has attempted to influence
   the administrative action of that agency. If the lobbyist is
   attempting to influence a bill before the legislature the
   lobbyist need only disclose the "Legislature" as the agency
   involved.

2. When a lobbyist presents a credit card for payment of a general
   lobbying expense the lobbyist has merely incurred an expense.
   Payment of the expense is not made until payment is made to the
   credit company. Accordingly a lobbyist may disclose general
   lobbying expenses on the report when payment is made to the
   credit company.

If you have any questions regarding the above advice please contact me.

**Memorandum**

To: Norm Fuller  
Franchise Tax Board

Date: July 23, 1976

From: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION  
Rich Eichman

Subject: Lobbyist Reporting

Recently, you presented the following statements to the Commission:

1. According to Section 86107(f), the agencies lobbied must be reported.

2. The definition of payment for credit card accounts is at the time the charge is made. Therefore, the incurrence and payment of the expense are at the same date and the filer has no choice as to when to report it.

The staff has discussed the above statements and reached the following conclusions with respect to each:

1. A lobbyist must disclose an agency in "Agency Involved", Form 620 or 625, only when the lobbyist has attempted to influence the administrative action of that agency. If the lobbyist is attempting to influence a bill before the legislature the lobbyist need only disclose the "Legislature" as the agency involved.

2. When a lobbyist presents a credit card for payment of a general lobbying expense the lobbyist has merely incurred an expense. Payment of the expense is not made until payment is made to the credit company. Accordingly a lobbyist may disclose general lobbying expenses on the report when payment is made to the credit company.

To: Norm Fuller  
Franchise Tax Board

Date: July 23, 1976

From: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION  
Rich Eichman

Subject: Lobbyist Reporting

Recently, you presented the following statements to the Commission:

1. According to Section 86107(f), the agencies lobbied must be reported.

2. The definition of payment for credit card accounts is at the time the charge is made. Therefore, the incurrence and payment of the expense are at the same date and the filer has no choice as to when to report it.

The staff has discussed the above statements and reached the following conclusions with respect to each:

1. A lobbyist must disclose an agency in "Agency Involved", Form 620 or 625, only when the lobbyist has attempted to influence the administrative action of that agency. If the lobbyist is attempting to influence a bill before the legislature the lobbyist need only disclose the "Legislature" as the agency involved.

2. When a lobbyist presents a credit card for payment of a general lobbying expense the lobbyist has merely incurred an expense. Payment of the expense is not made until payment is made to the credit company. Accordingly a lobbyist may disclose general lobbying expenses on the report when payment is made to the credit company.
